findings.title: Shell command injection via subprocess with user-controlled input
findings.description: The `_create_issue` function constructs a `gh` CLI command using `finding.title`, `finding.description`, `finding.suggestion`, and `config.labels` — all of which originate from AI-generated content. These values are passed directly as arguments to `subprocess.run` via a list, which is generally safe, but the content is inserted into `--body` and `--title` without sanitization. A malicious AI response could craft a `finding.title` containing shell metacharacters that, combined with the `gh` CLI parsing, could cause unintended behavior.
findings.suggestion: Validate or truncate AI-generated strings before passing to subprocess. At minimum, add length limits and character allowlists for titles and labels.

findings.title: MCP server executes `run_audit` without authentication or rate limiting
findings.description: The `run_audit` MCP tool allows any connected MCP client to trigger a full AI audit, which makes real API calls and incurs real cost. There is no authentication on the MCP server, no rate limiting, and no confirmation step. A compromised or malicious MCP client (e.g., a rogue Cursor plugin) could trigger unlimited expensive API calls.
findings.suggestion: Add a configuration option to disable the `run_audit` tool in the MCP server, or require explicit user confirmation before invoking it. Document the risk in the MCP server docs.

findings.title: Path traversal risk: MCP `record_decision` writes to hardcoded path without validation
findings.description: The `record_decision` tool in the MCP server calls `save_decision(".noxaudit/decisions.jsonl", decision)` with a hardcoded path. While the path is not user-controlled here, the `load_latest_findings()` call uses a relative path from cwd. If the MCP server is started from an unexpected working directory, the decisions file could be written to an unintended location.
findings.suggestion: Use an absolute path resolved at server startup (e.g., from config), not a hardcoded relative path.

findings.title: Telegram bot token logged in warning message on failure
findings.description: When the Telegram API returns a non-200 status, the full response body is printed: `print(f"Warning: Telegram API returned {resp.status_code}: {resp.text}")`. Telegram error responses can include the bot token or partial credentials in the response body in some error scenarios.
findings.suggestion: Limit the error output to the status code and a safe truncation of the response body, e.g., `resp.text[:200]`.

findings.title: JSON parsing without error handling can crash on malformed AI responses
findings.description: In `_parse_response`, `json.loads(text.strip())` is called without a try/except. If the AI returns malformed JSON (e.e., a rate-limit HTML page or partial response), this will raise an unhandled `json.JSONDecodeError` that propagates to the caller. The same issue exists in `gemini.py` and `openai.py`.
findings.suggestion: Wrap JSON parsing in a try/except JSONDecodeError and return an empty findings list with a warning on parse failure.

findings.title: JSON parsing without error handling in `_parse_text` and `_parse_response`
findings.description: Both `_parse_text` and `_parse_response` in GeminiProvider call `json.loads(text.strip())` without exception handling. Malformed responses from Gemini will cause an unhandled exception.
findings.suggestion: Add try/except around json.loads and return [] with a logged warning on failure.

findings.title: JSON parsing without error handling in `_parse_text`
findings.description: `_parse_text` in OpenAIProvider calls `json.loads(text.strip())` without exception handling. Malformed AI responses will cause unhandled exceptions.
findings.suggestion: Add try/except around json.loads and return [] with a logged warning on failure.

findings.title: GitHub Action installs noxaudit from a mutable git URL without pinning
findings.description: The action installs noxaudit via `pip install git+https://github.com/atriumn/noxaudit.git` without a commit SHA or tag. This means the `@main` branch tip is always installed, which is vulnerable to supply chain attacks if the repository is compromised — a malicious commit to main would be automatically pulled into all users' CI pipelines.
findings.suggestion: Pin to a specific tag or commit SHA: `pip install git+https://github.com/atriumn/noxaudit.git@v0.1.0` or publish to PyPI and install from there.

findings.title: API keys passed as environment variables may be visible in process listing
findings.description: The action passes `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`, and `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` as environment variables to the shell `run` step. While GitHub Actions masks these in logs, environment variables are visible to any process running in the same job (e.g., via `/proc/PID/environ` on Linux).
findings.suggestion: This is an accepted trade-off for GitHub Actions, but document that jobs sharing a runner with untrusted code should not use this action. Consider using GitHub's OIDC for providers that support it.

findings.title: Path traversal via `os.path.expanduser` on repo path from config
findings.description: The `path` field from `noxaudit.yml` is processed with `os.path.expanduser()` but not validated to be within any expected boundary. A malicious config file (e.g., in a shared environment) could point the repo path at sensitive directories like `/etc` or `~/.ssh`.
findings.suggestion: Validate that the expanded path is a directory that actually exists and optionally restrict it to be within the project root or a configurable base directory.

findings.title: GitHub issue creation fails open: `_issue_exists` returns False on all errors
findings.description: `_issue_exists` catches `subprocess.TimeoutExpired`, `OSError`, and `json.JSONDecodeError` and returns `False` in all cases. This means if the `gh` CLI times out or returns an error, the code assumes no issue exists and creates a duplicate issue.
findings.suggestion: Return `True` (assume issue exists) on timeout or error to be safe, or raise the error and let the caller decide whether to skip issue creation.

findings.title: Module docstring references old project name 'Nightwatch'
findings.description: The module docstring says `"""Nightwatch: Nightly AI-powered codebase audits."""` but the project was renamed to Noxaudit.
findings.suggestion: Update to `"""Noxaudit: Nightly AI-powered codebase audits."""`

findings.title: Report title references old project name 'Nightwatch'
findings.description: `generate_report` produces a title `# Nightwatch Report: {focus}` but the project is named Noxaudit.
findings.suggestion: Change to `# Noxaudit Report: {focus}`

findings.title: README lists 'testing' as a focus area in one table but omits it in another
findings.description: The 'Focus Areas' table in README.md lists 6 areas (security, docs, patterns, performance, hygiene, dependencies) and omits 'testing', but the code has 7 focus areas including testing. The 'How It Works' section says 'Default is all 7 focus areas' which contradicts the table.
findings.suggestion: Add 'testing' to the Focus Areas table in README.md to match the actual 7 focus areas in the codebase.

findings.title: Configuration reference shows wrong default for `provider_rotation`
findings.description: The configuration reference table states `repos[].provider_rotation` defaults to `[gemini]`, but `config.py` `RepoConfig` sets the default to `["gemini"]` while the docs guide and examples use `[anthropic]`. This creates confusion about what the actual default is.
findings.suggestion: Ensure the default in the reference table matches the code default in `RepoConfig`.

findings.title: CHANGELOG version 0.1.0 is dated 2026-02-15 (future date)
findings.description: The initial release is dated 2026-02-15, which is a future date relative to typical development timelines. This may indicate a placeholder or error.
findings.suggestion: Verify and correct the release date to the actual release date.

findings.title: Typo in decisions guide: missing space before 'even'
findings.description: The sentence reads: 'the decision is invalidated and the finding resurfaceeven if the decision hasn't expired.' — missing a space between 'resurface' and 'even'.
findings.suggestion: Fix to: 'the decision is invalidated and the finding resurfaces even if the decision hasn't expired.'

findings.title: Provider reference lists `gpt-5.2` output price as $14.00/M but README says $7.00/M
findings.description: The providers reference table shows `gpt-5.2` at $14.00/M output, but the README's supported models table shows $7.00/M output for the same model. These are inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Reconcile the two tables to show the same price, verifying against the actual OpenAI pricing.

findings.title: First audit walkthrough references `claude-sonnet-4-5` but estimate shows `claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929`
findings.description: The estimate output in `first-audit.md` shows `claude-sonnet-4-5` as the provider model, but the configuration reference and code default to `claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929`. This minor inconsistency may confuse users.
findings.suggestion: Use the full model identifier consistently in documentation examples.

findings.title: GeminiProvider and OpenAIProvider not available in `_retrieve_repo` if import failed
findings.description: In `runner.py`, `PROVIDERS` is populated at module load time. `GeminiProvider` and `OpenAIProvider` are imported at the top, but if the optional dependencies are not installed, only `AnthropicProvider` is available. However, the `GeminiProvider` import is unconditional at the top of the file (`from noxaudit.providers.gemini import GeminiProvider`), which will raise an `ImportError` at module import time if `google-genai` is not installed, breaking the entire CLI even for Anthropic-only users.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the GeminiProvider import in a try/except ImportError at the top of runner.py, similar to how OpenAIProvider is handled.

findings.title: Duplicate code between `_run_repo_sync` and `_submit_repo`/`_retrieve_repo`
findings.description: `_run_repo_sync` duplicates significant logic from `_submit_repo` and `_retrieve_repo`: file gathering, provider resolution, prepass check, decision loading, report generation, notification sending, and issue creation. Any change to one path must be mirrored in the other, creating maintenance risk.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to extract shared logic into helper functions, or have `run_audit` internally call `submit_audit` + `retrieve_audit` in sequence.

findings.title: Duplicated `_build_user_message`, `_format_files`, and `_make_finding_id` across all three providers
findings.description: The methods `_build_user_message`, `_format_files`, and `_make_finding_id` are implemented identically in `anthropic.py`, `gemini.py`, and `openai.py`. The `FINDING_SCHEMA` dict is also duplicated across all three files.
findings.suggestion: Move these shared methods to `BaseProvider` in `providers/base.py` so they are defined once and inherited.

findings.title: `gather_files` in `BaseFocus` is duplicated by `gather_files_combined` top-level function
findings.description: `BaseFocus.gather_files` and the module-level `gather_files_combined` share almost identical logic for filtering excluded paths, skipping large files, and reading file content. The runner only uses `gather_files_combined`, making `BaseFocus.gather_files` effectively dead code.
findings.suggestion: Remove `BaseFocus.gather_files` or have it delegate to `gather_files_combined([self], repo_path, exclude_patterns)` to eliminate duplication.

findings.title: Inconsistent import style: some imports inside functions, others at module level
findings.description: Several functions in `cli.py` import modules inside the function body (e.g., `from noxaudit.pricing import ...` inside `estimate`, `from noxaudit.mcp.state import load_latest_findings` inside `_load_findings_for_baseline`). The dominant pattern in the codebase is top-level imports.
findings.suggestion: Move function-local imports to the top of the file unless there is a specific reason to defer them (e.g., optional dependency).

findings.title: Missing test for `retrieve_audit` with Anthropic provider (only Gemini batch is tested)
findings.description: The CLI integration tests cover Gemini's submit/retrieve flow (`TestGeminiBatchCLIIntegration`) but there are no corresponding integration tests for the Anthropic batch submit/retrieve workflow end-to-end through the CLI.
findings.suggestion: Add a `TestAnthropicBatchCLIIntegration` class mirroring `TestGeminiBatchCLIIntegration` to ensure the Anthropic path stays wired correctly.

findings.title: No tests for Telegram notification module
findings.description: `noxaudit/notifications/telegram.py` has zero test coverage. The `send_telegram` function has several branches (missing token, missing chat_id, non-200 response) that are untested.
findings.suggestion: Add tests using `httpx` mocking to cover the happy path and all error branches.

findings.title: No unit tests for `prepass.py` core logic
findings.description: `enrich_files`, `run_prepass`, `build_classification_prompt`, and `_severity_to_tier` in `prepass.py` have no direct unit tests. The prepass is only tested indirectly via CLI integration tests.
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for `enrich_files` (verify FULL/SNIPPET/MAP/SKIP tiers produce correct output), `_severity_to_tier`, and `build_classification_prompt`.

findings.title: No tests for GitHub issue creation module
findings.description: `noxaudit/issues.py` has no test coverage. The `create_issues_for_findings`, `_issue_exists`, and `_create_issue` functions are untested.
findings.suggestion: Add tests mocking `subprocess.run` to cover issue creation, deduplication via `_issue_exists`, severity threshold filtering, and error handling.

findings.title: Missing test for `format_notification` with 'testing' focus area icon
findings.description: The `format_notification` function uses a `focus_icons` dict that does not include an entry for 'testing', so it falls back to the generic '🔍' icon. The test `test_single_focus_icon` only tests 'security'. The missing 'testing' entry is a silent gap.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that verifies the 'testing' focus area falls back to the generic icon and that the displayed focus name is correct.

findings.title: Unconditional top-level import of GeminiProvider breaks CLI when google-genai not installed
findings.description: `runner.py` imports `from noxaudit.providers.gemini import GeminiProvider` unconditionally at the top level. `gemini.py` itself guards with a try/except on `import google.genai`, setting `genai = None` on failure, but `GeminiProvider` class is still defined — however the module import of runner.py will succeed. The problem is that `GeminiProvider` is then added to `PROVIDERS` dict unconditionally, so users without google-genai who specify `provider: gemini` will get a runtime `ImportError` only when they try to instantiate it, rather than a clear error at startup.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the GeminiProvider import in runner.py with a try/except, similar to OpenAIProvider, for consistency and clear error messaging.

findings.title: `BaseFocus.gather_files` method is dead code — never called by the runner
findings.description: The `gather_files` instance method on `BaseFocus` is defined but the runner exclusively uses the module-level `gather_files_combined` function. No code in the codebase calls `focus_instance.gather_files(...)`. This is dead code that could mislead contributors.
findings.suggestion: Either remove `gather_files` from `BaseFocus` or have `gather_files_combined` delegate to it. If removing, check that no external consumers use the method.

findings.title: Roadmap doc references non-existent internal files
findings.description: The roadmap doc references `03.5-saas-application.md` and `06-product-quality-focus-areas.md` (e.g., 'See [SaaS Application](03.5-saas-application.md)') but these files are not present in the provided codebase, creating broken internal links.
findings.suggestion: Remove or stub out the broken cross-references, or create placeholder files for the referenced documents.

findings.title: Duplicate `dev` dependency group defined in both `[project.optional-dependencies]` and `[dependency-groups]`
findings.description: `pyproject.toml` defines `[project.optional-dependencies] dev` with `jsonschema`, `pre-commit`, `pytest`, `ruff` AND a separate `[dependency-groups] dev` with only `pytest` and `ruff`. These two groups will conflict and the `[dependency-groups]` section will shadow or be ignored depending on the tool used.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `[dependency-groups]` section and consolidate all dev dependencies in `[project.optional-dependencies.dev]`.

findings.title: Inconsistent pytest version pinning between dev extras
findings.description: `[project.optional-dependencies] dev` pins `pytest>=8.0` but `[dependency-groups] dev` pins `pytest>=9.0.2`. These are different minimum versions for the same package in the same project.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate to a single dev dependency definition with a consistent version requirement.

findings.title: Inconsistent ruff version pinning between dev extras
findings.description: `[project.optional-dependencies] dev` pins `ruff>=0.8` but `[dependency-groups] dev` pins `ruff>=0.15.1`. The `.pre-commit-config.yaml` uses `v0.15.1`. These are inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Align all ruff version references to `>=0.15.1` and remove the duplicate dependency group.

findings.title: MODEL_PRICING contains speculative future model names (`gemini-3-flash`, `gpt-5`, `gpt-5.2`, `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5-nano`)
findings.description: The `MODEL_PRICING` dict and provider reference docs include models like `gemini-3-flash`, `gpt-5`, `gpt-5.2`, `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5-nano` that appear to be speculative/aspirational model names not yet released at the time of writing. If these model identifiers don't match actual API model names, users will get silent fallbacks via `resolve_model_key`.
findings.suggestion: Clearly mark speculative model entries as aspirational in comments, or remove them until they are publicly available with confirmed model IDs.

findings.title: Unbounded file read in `load_decisions` — no size limit on decisions.jsonl
findings.description: `load_decisions` reads the entire decisions file into memory with `path.read_text().strip().splitlines()`. In a long-running project, the decisions file could grow very large. Similarly, `remove_baseline_decisions` rewrites the entire file atomically, which is not atomic (no temp file + rename pattern) and could corrupt the file on write failure.
findings.suggestion: Use a streaming read approach for large files and use atomic writes (write to temp file, then rename) to prevent corruption.

findings.title: CostLedger reads entire ledger file on every `get_last_n_days` call
findings.description: `get_last_n_days` calls `read_entries()` which reads and parses the entire JSONL file. Since `status` command calls this every time it's invoked, and the ledger grows unboundedly, this will become slow over time.
findings.suggestion: Consider reading the file in reverse (last N entries) for `get_last_n` operations, or adding periodic compaction of old entries.

findings.title: `gather_files_combined` performs O(n*m) `any(ex in rel ...)` check for each file
findings.description: For each file path, `gather_files_combined` checks `any(ex in rel for ex in exclude)`. If `exclude` is large or file paths are long, this is O(files × exclude_patterns). For large repos with many exclude patterns, this could be slow.
findings.suggestion: Compile exclude patterns into a more efficient check, e.g., split path components and check set membership rather than substring matching on the full path string.
